question:
Produce a concise system history (in engineering report/narrative form) of the Tekoa WWTP (collection + I&I) from ~2006 to present.
Emphasize: the 2006 river-influence/flow study, monitored hot spots (A25/B7N/D1/F1N),
the 2018 sewer improvements, the influent lift-station Basis of Design (~404 gpm,
siphon abandonment), and the 2022–2024 peak spikes.;
cite (Title, page/figure) inline.

nodes.text:
# Tekoa WWTP — I&I and System History (2006 → Present)

----

### 2006–2010  
**I&I & River Influence Study:**  
* Hangman Creek stage ↔ sewer flows  
* Floodplain/high groundwater drives infiltration  
* Seasonal pattern: late winter/spring  

----

### 2017 Monitoring (5-min at D1, B7N, A25, F1N):  
* Sharp RDII pulses; D1/B7N surcharge  
* Weirs maxed; AV velocity methods  
* 4 basins ~69% of I/I (~45% ERUs)  
* Spring WWTP flows > design  

----

### 2018 Targeted Improvements:  
* Relocate mains from trashed lids  
* Rehab brick MHs near seasonal streams  
* Focus on D-line / Crosby–Ramsey  

----

### 2019–2021 DMR:  
* No daily >0.9 MGD  
* AADF ≈ 0.11–0.12 MGD  
* Seasonal I&I without extremes  

----

### 2020–2021 Lift Station DMR:  
* Design PHF ≈ ADWF × PF(3) + I&I → ~404 gpm  
* Abandon 10" siphon (likely major I&I)  
* New 12" C900 inlet; Triplex VFD  
* Two pumps ≈ 650 gpm total  

----

### 2022–2024 DMR:  
* Emergence of daily spikes > 0.9 MGD  
* Align with rainfall; consistent with 2017 RDII behavior  
* Suggests persistent hotspot basins & storm clusters  

----

### 2024–2025 Characterization:  
* Flat AADF (OFM-based)  
* RDII top events ranked  
* Synthetic hourly hydrographs  
* EQ envelope for Q_allow 180–240 gpm  

----

Hotspot Basins: A25, B7N, D1, F1N | River Influence: Hangman Creek stage ↔ infiltration  

Design Considerations: Biological at MWDF/MDDF | Pass 100% PHDF/PIDF | Consider EQ & targeted I/I
nodes.text_raw:
# Tekoa WWTP — I&I and System History (2006 → Present)

----

### 2006–2010  
**I&I & River Influence Study:**  
* Hangman Creek stage ↔ sewer flows  
* Floodplain/high groundwater drives infiltration  
* Seasonal pattern: late winter/spring  

----

### 2017 Monitoring (5-min at D1, B7N, A25, F1N):  
* Sharp RDII pulses; D1/B7N surcharge  
* Weirs maxed; AV velocity methods  
* 4 basins ~69% of I/I (~45% ERUs)  
* Spring WWTP flows > design  

----

### 2018 Targeted Improvements:  
* Relocate mains from trashed lids  
* Rehab brick MHs near seasonal streams  
* Focus on D-line / Crosby–Ramsey  

----

### 2019–2021 DMR:  
* No daily >0.9 MGD  
* AADF ≈ 0.11–0.12 MGD  
* Seasonal I&I without extremes  

----

### 2020–2021 Lift Station DMR:  
* Design PHF ≈ ADWF × PF(3) + I&I → ~404 gpm  
* Abandon 10" siphon (likely major I&I)  
* New 12" C900 inlet; Triplex VFD  
* Two pumps ≈ 650 gpm total  

----

### 2022–2024 DMR:  
* Emergence of daily spikes > 0.9 MGD  
* Align with rainfall; consistent with 2017 RDII behavior  
* Suggests persistent hotspot basins & storm clusters  

----

### 2024–2025 Characterization:  
* Flat AADF (OFM-based)  
* RDII top events ranked  
* Synthetic hourly hydrographs  
* EQ envelope for Q_allow 180–240 gpm  

----

Hotspot Basins: A25, B7N, D1, F1N | River Influence: Hangman Creek stage ↔ infiltration  

Design Considerations: Biological at MWDF/MDDF | Pass 100% PHDF/PIDF | Consider EQ & targeted I/I

nodes.text:
| Year/Period                  | Event/Study                                                      | Source                                                                         | Key Findings / Notes                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | I\&I Impact                                                                                                                      |
| ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 2006–2010                    | Citywide I\&I Study; River Influence Analysis                    | 2006-10 Infiltration & Inflow Study (PDF)                                      | Hangman (Latah) Creek stage correlates with sewer flows; floodplain and high groundwater drive infiltration in nearby corridors. Established the long-term seasonal pattern (late winter/spring wet-weather elevations).                                                                              | Baseline confirmation that creek/groundwater materially elevate I\&I in specific reaches.                                        |
| 2017 (Feb–Mar)               | Concurrent Flow Monitoring (D1, B7N, A25, F1N)                   | Appendix 3 U16-109 Tekoa WA Final Report; Final Combined I-I Evaluation (PDFs) | 5-min logging; multiple March storms produced sharp RDII pulses. D1 & B7N showed surcharging; weirs maxed, velocity formulas used. A25 & F1N more linear responses. Four monitored areas \~69% of I/I while serving \~45% of ERUs. Average monthly flows exceed plant capacity in late winter/spring. | Confirmed few basins dominate RDII; documented backwater/surcharging under peaks.                                                |
| 2018 (Design)                | Targeted Sewer Improvements (corridor relocations/manhole rehab) | Figure 2 & Figure 3 — 2018 Sewer Improvements (PDFs)                           | Design plans to move sewers out of marshy/athletic fields and seasonal stream alignments; replace/rehab brick manholes in wet areas, especially D-line near Crosby/Ramsey.                                                                                                                            | Expected to reduce chronic infiltration in identified hot spots if fully implemented.                                            |
| 2020–2021                    | Influent Lift Station Basis of Design                            | Basis of Design City of Tekoa Influent Lift Station Replacement (PDF)          | Design PHF derived as: ADWF × PF(=3) + I\&I; then compounded 1%/yr to \~404 gpm (20-yr). Old 10-inch siphon (major I\&I source) to be abandoned; new 12-inch C900 inlet. Triplex VFD station; two pumps in parallel up to \~650 gpm.                                                                  | Legacy design peak explains \~400+ gpm ‘design’ values even if daily averages were lower. Reduces some I\&I with siphon removal. |
| 2019–2021 (DMR)              | Daily Influent & Rainfall (DMR DB) — no extreme spikes           | dmr\_database.db                                                               | No days >0.9 MGD observed in 2019–2021; AADF \~0.11–0.12 MGD; seasonal wet-weather increases but not extreme in daily totals.                                                                                                                                                                         | Demonstrates ‘typical’ seasonal I\&I without extreme daily peaks in this interval.                                               |
| 2022–2024 (DMR)              | Daily Influent & Rainfall — emergence of spikes                  | dmr\_database.db                                                               | A few days each year exceed 0.9 MGD (daily total). Spike days align with rainfall; consistent with RDII pulses documented in 2017 but now evident in daily totals.                                                                                                                                    | Confirms episodic high-magnitude RDII still present; likely basin- and storm-dependent.                                          |
| 2024–2025 (Current Analysis) | System characterization; RDII event ranking; EQ envelope         | Characterization workbook + DMR DB                                             | OFM-based population (flat AADF); design flows validated by DB. Top RDII events computed; per-event hydrographs (synthetic hourly) developed from Tekoa diurnal patterns. EQ envelopes produced for Q\_allow caps (180–240 gpm).                                                                      | Provides a direct, data-driven basis to size EQ and prioritize I/I reduction in top basins.                                      |

nodes.text:
# 5.3 Wastewater Flow Summary *WAC 173-240-050(3)(g)* Discharge Monitoring Reports (DMRs) from 2022 through 2024 were analyzed to determine historical influent flows to WWTP for the design period. This data, which represents actual measured flows to and from the facility (taken daily), provides the basis for characterizing system hydraulics in this report. The flow data along with OFM population data, was used to generate **Table 5.3-1**, which summarizes total system flows, average daily flows, estimated total Equivalent Residential Units (ERUs), and the average daily flow per ERU. The table is followed by a
Year Total Flow Entire System
(MG/Year)		 Average Daily Flow (ADF)
(gpd)		 Total Equivalent Residential Units (ERU) Average Daily Flow Per ERU
(gpd/ERU)
2022 49.1 134,520 460 292
2023 42.87 117,452 460 255
2024 36.8 100,486 461 218
Guidance for ERU estimation and flow analysis was taken from {{ Cite_Guidance_Document, for instance, Washington State Department of Ecology's "Criteria for Sewage Works Design" (Orange Book) }}. The Orange Book provides standardized methodologies and typical values used in Washington State, ensuring consistency in engineering assessments. <> The average annual flow to the WWTP over this period was calculated to be {{ Calculated_Average_Annual_Flow }} gpd. This value is utilized for long-term operational planning, estimating annual O&M costs, and as a fundamental baseline for developing flow projections and assessing overall plant loading against its design capacity.

spore.reason: Cites WAC 173-240-050; Section: 5.3 Wastewater Flow Summary; p.69; WWTP guidance

nodes.text:
Tekoa’s WWTP began as a trickling filter system in 1950 and was converted to an activated sludge process in 1974, and has successfully served the community for decades. However, despite various upgrades over time, ongoing assessment and long-term planning remain crucial for the facility's continued effectiveness. A comprehensive sewer plan was completed in 2006, and subsequent efforts have focused on addressing significant operational challenges stemming from significant infiltration and inflow issues.

Since the 2006 general sewer plan, continued upgrades and planning resulting from the system’s persistent performance issues primarily stem from severe infiltration and inflow (I&I) within the aged collection system. Until recently, excessive I&I lead to influent flows, impacting treatment efficiency and resulting in violations. Effective management of residual solids, which are aerobically digested and dewatered, also remains an important operational consideration.

The present planning effort aims to establish a foundation for sustained regulatory compliance, particularly in response to recent changes in Total Maximum Daily Load (TMDL) requirements for nutrients and temperature in Hangman Creek discussed in chapter 2. Addressing community needs and improving overall operational efficiency are also long-term goals. The 2006 sewer plan and subsequent actions have prioritized resolving the I&I issues as a critical step before definitive upgrades to the treatment plant itself can be accurately planned and implemented.

Specific areas identified for past and ongoing evaluation and improvement either by town staff, previous reports, or NPDES permit WA0023141 include:

* Extensive rehabilitation of the collection system to reduce I&I, including pipe and manhole replacements. Evidence of this effort being largely successful in achieving compliance is presented later in this report, though some inflow and infiltration issues remain.

* Optimization of the existing activated sludge process components once hydraulic loadings are better managed.

* Effective solids management and removal strategies.

* Evaluation of advanced or tertiary treatment processes may be necessary to meet stringent nutrient limits and further protect Hangman Creek, potentially reducing or altering discharge characteristics during critical periods.

As a basis for planning for the continued improvements noted above, the following information is presented in chapters 2 through 4:

* A thorough summary of the necessary background information required to perform a well-informed alternatives analysis specific to the needs of the Tekoa community.

* An evaluation of the condition and capacity of the existing wastewater collection and treatment facilities.

# CITY OF TEKOA

## SEWER LEGEND

* CITY LIMITS/SERVICE AREA
* MANHOLE (FOUND)
* MANHOLE (NOT FOUND)
* SEWER MAIN (GRAVITY)
* AREA SERVED BY CITY
* OUTSIDE CITY LIMITS
* PREVIOUS 2005 SEWER IMPROVEMENTS
* PREVIOUS 2008 SEWER IMPROVEMENTS
* PREVIOUS 2013 SEWER IMPROVEMENTS
* PREVIOUS 2014 SEWER IMPROVEMENTS
* PROPOSED 2018 SEWER IMPROVEMENTS

----

### Proposed Future Improvements

* Treatment Plant to be upgraded.

----

### Map Annotations (Selected)

- **Treatment Plant** and **Lift Station** marked near the southwest corner.
- Streets labeled include:
  - Garfield, Cannon, Harrison, Madison, Taft, Wall, Lincoln, Jackson, Sherman, Custer, Alder, Sheridan, Washington, North Ramsey, North Cross, Broadway, North Road, Poplar, Idaho, Seamon Rd, Connell, Warren, Crosby.
- Areas labeled:
  - Byron, Holmes, Spring, Cecil Park, Reservoir.
- Sewer lines and manholes marked with various codes (e.g., C9.1, D3.1A, B4.1(LH), etc.).
- Sewer improvements indicated by colored lines:
  - Previous 2005 (green)
  - Previous 2008 (orange)
  - Previous 2013 (blue)
  - Previous 2014 (red)
  - Proposed 2018 (yellow)

----

### Engineering Firm

**CENTURY WEST ENGINEERING**

----

### Notes on Map

- City crews to replace sewer in certain areas.
- Replace sewer and relocate to right of way in specified sections.

----

### Sheet Information

- Sheet 1 of 2

nodes.text:
Text from WAC 173-240-060 Inclusion Check Confirmation / Location
the project-specific decisions.
A location map of the project area is included.
A map showing the current and proposed sewer service areas is included.
At least one map is scaled to show the complete, current, and proposed service areas along with the relationship of this service area to adjacent service areas.
A map showing the existing collection system changes and proposed locations of land applications of wastewater is included. (If applicable)
Office of Financial Management (OFM) or other population data for the service area for at least the last two decades is provided (if available).
Population projections for the project planning area and concentrated growth areas for the project design period are included.
Population projections are based on historical records cited in the approved general sewer plan and are consistent with data from appropriate local land use planning agencies.
(c) A statement of the present and expected future quantity and quality of wastewater, including any industrial wastes which may be present or expected in the sewer system. The analysis of current conditions includes characterization of waste loading.
Any constituents that may have a detrimental impact on proposed unit processes are identified and discussed.
The distribution of wastewater sources (domestic, commercial, industrial) is included, ideally expressed as percentages.
Characteristics of industrial discharges and any pretreatment requirements are discussed. (If applicable)
Current I/I flows are included.
Flows received from combined sewers are included and assessed. (If applicable)
An assessment of diurnal flow and loading variations is included.
An assessment of seasonal load and flow variations is included.
